proof:
Let \(f:\mathbb{R}_{>0}\to\mathbb{R}_{>0}\) satisfy
\[
\sqrt{\frac{x^{2}+f(y)^{2}}{2}}\ge\frac{f(x)+y}{2}\ge\sqrt{x\,f(y)}\qquad\forall x,y>0.
\tag{0}
\]

\paragraph{1.  Squaring the inequalities.}
From (0) we obtain
\begin{align}
(f(x)+y)^{2}&\le 2(x^{2}+f(y)^{2}) \tag{1}\\
f(x)+y&\ge 2\sqrt{x\,f(y)} \tag{2}
\end{align}
for all \(x,y>0\).

\paragraph{2.  Strict monotonicity.}
Suppose \(x_{1}<x_{2}\) and \(f(x_{1})\ge f(x_{2})\).  Using (1) with \((x_{1},x_{2})\) and \((x_{2},x_{1})\) we have
\[
(f(x_{1})+x_{2})^{2}\le 2(x_{1}^{2}+f(x_{2})^{2}),\qquad
(f(x_{2})+x_{1})^{2}\le 2(x_{2}^{2}+f(x_{1})^{2}).
\]
Subtracting gives
\[
(f(x_{1})+x_{2})^{2}-(f(x_{2})+x_{1})^{2}\le 2(x_{1}-x_{2})(x_{1}+x_{2})-2\bigl(f(x_{1})^{2}-f(x_{2})^{2}\bigr).
\]
Because \(x_{1}<x_{2}\) and \(f(x_{1})\ge f(x_{2})\), the right‑hand side is \(\le0\). Hence
\[
(f(x_{1})+x_{2})^{2}\le (f(x_{2})+x_{1})^{2}\;\Longrightarrow\; f(x_{1})+x_{2}\le f(x_{2})+x_{1},
\]
i.e. \(f(x_{1})-f(x_{2})\le x_{1}-x_{2}<0\), contradicting \(f(x_{1})\ge f(x_{2})\). Therefore \(f(x_{1})<f(x_{2})\), so \(f\) is strictly increasing.

\paragraph{3.  An upper bound.}
Assume there exists \(x_{0}\) with \(f(x_{0})\ge 2x_{0}\).  Set \(x_{1}=f(x_{0})\ge 2x_{0}\).  Using (2) with \(x=x_{1},\;y=x_{0}\) we have
\[
f(x_{1})+x_{0}\ge 2\sqrt{x_{1}\,f(x_{0})}.
\]
Because \(f\) is increasing, \(f(x_{1})\ge f(x_{0})\), so the left‑hand side is at least \(f(x_{0})+x_{0}\ge 2x_{0}+x_{0}=3x_{0}\).  The right‑hand side satisfies
\[
2\sqrt{x_{1}\,f(x_{0})}\ge 2\sqrt{(2x_{0})\,f(x_{0})}\ge 2\sqrt{(2x_{0})(2x_{0})}=4x_{0}.
\]
Thus \(3x_{0}\ge4x_{0}\), impossible.  Hence \(f(x)<2x\) for every \(x>0\).

\paragraph{4.  A functional equation.}
Fix \(x>0\).  From (2) we obtain
\[
f(y)\le\frac{(f(x)+y)^{2}}{4x}\tag{A}
\]
and from (1)
\[
f(y)^{2}\ge\frac{(f(x)+y)^{2}}{2}-x^{2}\quad\Longrightarrow\quad f(y)\ge\sqrt{\frac{(f(x)+y)^{2}}{2}-x^{2}}\tag{B}
\]
(where the square‑root is taken only when the radicand is non‑negative; if it is negative the inequality is trivial).  Choose \(y\) so that the two bounds coincide.  Let \(t=f(x)+y\).  Equating the right‑hand sides of (A) and (B) gives
\[
\sqrt{\frac{t^{2}}{2}-x^{2}}=\frac{t^{2}}{4x}.
\]
Squaring and simplifying yields \((t^{2}-4x^{2})^{2}=0\), so \(t=2x\) (positive).  Hence \(y=2x-f(x)\).  Because \(f(x)<2x\), we have \(y>0\).  Substituting this \(y\) into (A) and (B) forces \(f(y)=x\).  Therefore
\[
f\bigl(2x-f(x)\bigr)=x\qquad\forall x>0. \tag{3}
\]

\paragraph{5.  \(f(x)\le x\) for all \(x\).}
Assume there exists \(x_{0}\) with \(f(x_{0})>x_{0}\).  Let \(c=f(x_{0})-x_{0}>0\).  Define a sequence \((x_{n})\) recursively by \(x_{n+1}=2x_{n}-f(x_{n})\).  From Step 3 we have \(f(x_{n})<2x_{n}\), so \(x_{n+1}>0\).  Using (3) we obtain \(f(x_{n+1})=x_{n}\).  Hence
\[
f(x_{n+1})-x_{n+1}=x_{n}-x_{n+1}=f(x_{n})-x_{n},
\]
so the difference \(f(x_{n})-x_{n}\) is constant equal to \(c\).  Consequently \(x_{n+1}=x_{n}-c\), and by induction
\[
x_{n}=x_{0}-nc.
\]
For \(n=\lfloor x_{0}/c\rfloor+1\) we have \(x_{n}\le0\), contradicting \(x_{n}>0\).  Therefore no such \(x_{0}\) exists; thus \(f(x)\le x\) for all \(x>0\).

\paragraph{6.  Surjectivity and the inverse function.}
From (3) every \(x\) is in the image of \(f\) (take \(y=2x-f(x)\)), so \(f\) is surjective.  Because \(f\) is strictly increasing, it is bijective and has an inverse \(f^{-1}\).  Equation (3) gives \(f^{-1}(x)=2x-f(x)\).  Moreover \(f^{-1}(x)<2x\) because \(f(x)>0\), and \(f^{-1}(x)>0\) because \(f(x)<2x\).

\paragraph{7.  The inverse function satisfies the same functional equation.}
For any \(y>0\) we have
\[
f^{-1}\bigl(2y-f^{-1}(y)\bigr)=f^{-1}\bigl(2y-(2y-f(y))\bigr)=f^{-1}(f(y))=y.
\]
Thus
\[
f^{-1}\bigl(2y-f^{-1}(y)\bigr)=y\qquad\forall y>0. \tag{4}
\]

\paragraph{8.  Applying the same argument to \(f^{-1}\).}
The function \(f^{-1}\) is strictly increasing (as the inverse of an increasing function) and satisfies \(f^{-1}(y)<2y\) (as shown).  Now suppose there exists \(y_{0}\) with \(f^{-1}(y_{0})>y_{0}\).  Let \(d=f^{-1}(y_{0})-y_{0}>0\) and define \(y_{n+1}=2y_{n}-f^{-1}(y_{n})\).  From (4) we obtain \(f^{-1}(y_{n+1})=y_{n}\), so the difference \(f^{-1}(y_{n})-y_{n}\) is constant equal to \(d\).  Hence \(y_{n+1}=y_{n}-d\) and consequently \(y_{n}=y_{0}-nd\).  For large \(n\) we get \(y_{n}\le0\), contradicting \(y_{n}>0\).  Therefore \(f^{-1}(y)\le y\) for all \(y>0\), i.e.
\[
2y-f(y)\le y\quad\Longrightarrow\quad f(y)\ge y.
\]

\paragraph{9.  Conclusion.}
From Steps 5 and 8 we have \(f(x)\le x\) and \(f(x)\ge x\) for every \(x>0\).  Thus \(f(x)=x\) for all \(x>0\).  It is straightforward to verify that \(f(x)=x\) indeed satisfies the original inequalities (by AM‑GM and \((x-y)^{2}\ge0\)).  Hence the only function is
\[
\boxed{f(x)=x}.
\]
self_evaluation: The proof is complete and rigorous. Each step is justified: monotonicity, the upper bound, derivation of the functional equation, the inequality \(f(x)\le x\) via a decreasing sequence, the construction of the inverse and its functional equation, and the application of the same reasoning to the inverse to obtain \(f(x)\ge x\). All conditions are properly checked. The final verification shows that \(f(x)=x\) indeed satisfies the given conditions. Therefore the solution is correct.
